rows.answer: No, the caption should not call the harmonic (1,1) representative 'the rational Hodge class' unless it is explicitly known to be rational. A rational Hodge class is a class in the Hodge decomposition that can be represented by a rational cohomology class, while a harmonic form is merely a representative of a cohomology class that is harmonic with respect to a Kähler metric.

In the context of a smooth projective fourfold \( X \), a rational class \( \alpha \in H^4(X, \mathbb{Q}) \cap H^{2,2}(X) \) can indeed correspond to an algebraic cycle if it lies in the image of the cycle map from the Chow group of algebraic cycles. However, whether it can be represented by an algebraic cycle depends on additional geometric properties of \( X \) and the specific class \( \alpha \). The "register ladder" and "card supply" terminology is unclear in this context; if you meant to refer to specific conditions or conjectures in algebraic geometry, please clarify.

In the context of projective geometry, the body is the codimension-k linear subspace \( \mathbb{P}^{n-k} \), the shadow is the ambient space \( \mathbb{P}^n \), and the direction is defined by the inclusion of the subspace into the ambient space, typically represented by the projection from \( \mathbb{P}^n \) onto \( \mathbb{P}^{n-k} \).

In the context of Hodge theory, the first Hodge card typically uses the Hodge decomposition of the first cohomology group \( H^1 \) of a smooth projective curve. If the curve has rich \( H^1 \), it suggests that the structure of \( H^1 \) can provide significant information about the algebraic geometry of the curve. Therefore, using \( H^1 \) as the shadow in the first Hodge card is appropriate, as it reflects the interplay between the algebraic and topological aspects of the curve.

rows.target_register: R3->R4 in the known p=1 case
rows.answer: A rational (1,1) class on a smooth projective variety can be named as a Kähler class. It must not generalize to a non-Kähler class, as Kähler classes are characterized by their ability to be represented by a Kähler metric, which is not the case for all (1,1) classes on a projective variety.

Yes, the phenomenon of a rational (1,1) class on a smooth projective surface \( S \) being represented by a curve class can be seen as an extension of the classical Hodge theory related to divisors. While divisors correspond to the cohomology classes of (1,1) forms, the existence of rational (1,1) classes represented by curves indicates a richer structure in the Hodge decomposition, reflecting the interplay between geometry and topology on surfaces. This suggests that the Hodge structure can capture more than just the divisor classes, encompassing broader geometric configurations.

Yes, on a smooth projective threefold, if a rational (2,2) class is represented by a curve class, it can indeed be upgraded to an integral statement. Specifically, if the rational class is represented by a curve, then there exists an integral (2,2) class that is represented by an integral curve. This follows from the properties of the Néron-Severi group and the structure of the Chow ring in this context.

In the context of compact Kähler manifolds, the Hodge decomposition allows for the identification of harmonic representatives of cohomology classes. If the (p,p) class is rational, it can indeed be represented by a smooth projective variety, and the Hodge structure can be reused without altering the source row, as long as the underlying cohomology classes remain consistent. Thus, the generator can effectively utilize the smooth-projective Hodge card while preserving the integrity of the original cohomological structure.

Yes, the algebraicity of the locus where a fixed class of type (p,p) stays implies that this locus can be described by algebraic equations, which in turn can be used to construct an algebraic cycle body. Specifically, the algebraic cycles associated with this locus can be used to define a cycle class in the Chow ring, thereby contributing to the structure of the algebraic cycle body.

The answer key should clarify that the term 'cycle' typically refers to a 1-dimensional subvariety or a closed curve in the context of algebraic geometry, while a (1,1) class represents a cohomology class associated with divisors or line bundles. It may be more accurate to label the highlighted region as a 'divisor' or 'effective divisor' rather than 'the cycle.'
